content.text: Cost observability for agentic AI is the continuous tracking, attribution, and optimization of the financial costs incurred by agent operations. The primary cost driver is LLM inference — charged by the provider per input and output token — but agentic systems also incur costs from tool API calls, vector database queries, embedding compute, infrastructure (GPU/CPU), storage for agent memory, and network egress.

Unlike a web application where a page request costs microseconds of compute, a single agentic task can call an LLM dozens of times, invoke multiple external APIs, and run for minutes. A misconfigured agent loop that iterates 500 times instead of 5 can generate a bill hundreds of times larger than expected — in real time, with no automatic stop.
How much does this task cost right now?
Real-time cost tracking during agent execution.
Who or what is responsible for this cost?
Attribution to a user, session, agent type, task category, or team.
What can be done to reduce it?
Optimization insights — model choice, prompt compression, caching, loop guards.
Agentic AI systems are the first class of software where the cost of a single user request is

. A traditional API endpoint costs a fixed amount of compute; an agent can decide to loop, spawn sub-agents, or call expensive tools in an open-ended way. This makes cost a first-class reliability concern, not just a billing concern.
Without cost observability, teams face three failure modes:
An agent that enters a retry loop invokes the LLM hundreds of times. The first sign is a surprise invoice.
The team cannot price their product or calculate margins because they do not know the per-task cost.
A large LLM bill arrives with no visibility into which agent type, user cohort, or task category drove the spend.
— three properties required before any optimization or capacity planning is possible.
Per-session, per-agent, and per-user cost limits stop runaway spend before it reaches the invoice.
Cost per task type drives pricing decisions and product profitability modeling.
Comparing cost per task across models (e.g., haiku vs. sonnet vs. opus) reveals whether a cheaper model can handle the majority of tasks.
Input token cost attribution drives prompt engineers to remove filler context and tighten system prompts.
A cost spike at 2am triggers a PagerDuty alert rather than a Monday morning invoice surprise.
Engineering teams can charge individual product teams for their AI spend, enabling internal accountability.
To enforce a per-session budget in real time you need to estimate the cost of the next LLM call before making it. Provider tokenizers exist (e.g.,
via the API) but add latency to every call.

Provider invoices arrive hours to days after the call. Real-time cost tracking requires estimating cost from token counts and cached price tables, which must be kept up to date when providers change pricing.
Fine-grained attribution (per-user, per-task, per-step) requires attaching context metadata to every LLM call. Coarse attribution (per-agent-type only) is cheaper but provides less actionable signal.
External tool APIs do not report their cost in their response; you must maintain a cost model for each tool (e.g., web search costs $0.003 per call) and apply it at the call site.
Prompt caching (where the provider charges a reduced rate for cached prompt prefixes) changes the effective cost per call. Your cost model must account for cache hit rates.
Input tokens, output tokens × price per 1M tokens
Web search, code execution, data providers
RAG document indexing and query embedding

Real-time cost tracking computes cost in the agent's hot path, immediately after each LLM response, and updates a running total. This enables in-task budget enforcement — the agent can check the running total before each LLM call and refuse to proceed if the session limit would be exceeded.

Post-hoc attribution reads provider invoices or usage exports (typically daily or hourly) and joins them against internal metadata (task IDs, user IDs, agent types) stored during execution. Post-hoc attribution is accurate but delayed — you cannot use it to stop a runaway task mid-flight.
In production, run both: real-time tracking for enforcement and anomaly detection, post-hoc reconciliation to validate estimates against actual bills.

attrs.alt: Cost Observability — Agentic AI Cost Flow diagram showing Agent Runtime through Cost Middleware, Budget Gate, Metrics Store, and Dashboard with Circuit Breaker

content.text: Every LLM call passes through the Cost Middleware, which computes cost from the response's token counts and a cached price table. The Budget Gate checks the running session total against the configured limit. If the limit is not exceeded, the cost is forwarded to the Metrics Store as counters. The Dashboard and Alerting layer reads from the Metrics Store in real time. If the Budget Gate detects a limit breach, it signals the Circuit Breaker to halt the agent.
Any production agentic system where LLM costs are non-trivial — typically when monthly spend exceeds $100.
Multi-tenant platforms where different users or teams use the same agent infrastructure — attribution enables chargeback.
Systems with variable-length agent loops — the possibility of runaway iteration makes budget enforcement mandatory.
Before scaling an agent fleet — understanding per-task cost is required to project infrastructure spend at scale.
When evaluating model upgrades or downgrades — cost observability enables A/B cost comparison between model versions.

import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any
import anthropic


@dataclass
class CostLedger:
    """Accumulates cost for a single agent session."""
    session_id: str
    user_id: str
    agent_type: str
    budget_usd: float
    llm_cost_usd: float = 0.0
    tool_cost_usd: float = 0.0
    llm_calls: int = 0
    tool_calls: int = 0
    start_time: float = field(default_factory=time.time)

    @property
    def total_cost_usd(self) -> float:
        return self.llm_cost_usd + self.tool_cost_usd

    def check_budget(self) -> None:
        """Raise BudgetExceededError if total cost has exceeded the limit."""
        if self.total_cost_usd >= self.budget_usd:
            raise BudgetExceededError(
                f"Session {self.session_id} exceeded budget: "
                f"${self.total_cost_usd:.4f} >= ${self.budget_usd:.4f}"
            )

    def record_llm_call(self, model: str, input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> float:
        cost = compute_llm_cost(model, input_tokens, output_tokens)
        self.llm_cost_usd += cost
        self.llm_calls += 1
        return cost

    def record_tool_call(self, tool_name: str) -> float:
        cost = compute_tool_cost(tool_name)
        self.tool_cost_usd += cost
        self.tool_calls += 1
        return cost

    def as_dict(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "session_id": self.session_id,
            "user_id": self.user_id,
            "agent_type": self.agent_type,
            "llm_cost_usd": self.llm_cost_usd,
            "tool_cost_usd": self.tool_cost_usd,
            "total_cost_usd": self.total_cost_usd,
            "llm_calls": self.llm_calls,
            "tool_calls": self.tool_calls,
            "duration_seconds": round(time.time() - self.start_time, 2),
        }


class BudgetExceededError(RuntimeError):
    pass


class CostTrackingAgent:
    """Agent wrapper that tracks and enforces cost budgets."""

    def __init__(self, session_id: str, user_id: str, budget_usd: float = 0.50):
        self.client = anthropic.Anthropic()
        self.ledger = CostLedger(
            session_id=session_id,
            user_id=user_id,
            agent_type="research",
            budget_usd=budget_usd,
        )

    def call_llm(self, messages: list, tools: list, model="claude-haiku-4-5") -> Any:
        """Make an LLM call, record cost, enforce budget."""
        # Enforce budget BEFORE the call to avoid paying for a response we cannot use
        self.ledger.check_budget()

        response = self.client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=4096,
            tools=tools,
            messages=messages,
        )
        cost = self.ledger.record_llm_call(
            model=model,
            input_tokens=response.usage.input_tokens,
            output_tokens=response.usage.output_tokens,
        )
        # Re-check budget after recording the actual cost
        self.ledger.check_budget()
        return response

    def call_tool(self, tool_name: str, tool_fn, **kwargs) -> Any:
        """Execute a tool, record its cost."""
        self.ledger.check_budget()
        result = tool_fn(**kwargs)
        self.ledger.record_tool_call(tool_name)
        return result

from opentelemetry import metrics
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProvider
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.metric_exporter import OTLPMetricExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import PeriodicExportingMetricReader

# Setup OTel metrics pipeline
exporter = OTLPMetricExporter(endpoint="http://otel-collector:4317", insecure=True)
reader = PeriodicExportingMetricReader(exporter, export_interval_millis=15_000)
provider = MeterProvider(metric_readers=[reader])
metrics.set_meter_provider(provider)

meter = metrics.get_meter("agent.cost")

# Counters and histograms for cost observability
llm_cost_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "agent.llm.cost_usd",
    unit="USD",
    description="Cumulative LLM inference cost in USD",
)
tool_cost_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "agent.tool.cost_usd",
    unit="USD",
    description="Cumulative tool API cost in USD",
)
task_cost_histogram = meter.create_histogram(
    "agent.task.cost_usd",
    unit="USD",
    description="Per-task total cost distribution",
)


def emit_task_cost_metrics(ledger: CostLedger) -> None:
    """Publish cost metrics with attribution labels."""
    attrs = {
        "agent.type": ledger.agent_type,
        "user.id": ledger.user_id,   # fine-grained attribution
        "session.id": ledger.session_id,
    }
    llm_cost_counter.add(ledger.llm_cost_usd, attrs)
    tool_cost_counter.add(ledger.tool_cost_usd, attrs)
    task_cost_histogram.record(ledger.total_cost_usd, attrs)
A decorator pattern makes it easy to wrap any agent entrypoint with cost tracking and attribution without modifying the agent's internal logic:

A legal research startup built a document analysis agent that processed hundreds of contracts per day. After three weeks in production the monthly LLM invoice was $12,000 — four times their projection. Without cost observability they had no way to diagnose where the spend was coming from.

They added per-task cost tracking with attribution by task type (contract review vs. clause extraction vs. summary). The breakdown revealed that summary tasks, which they assumed were cheap, were generating 8,000–12,000 output tokens per run because the system prompt instructed the agent to include exhaustive citations. This single task type drove 68% of total spend.
The fix was to add a

cap for summary tasks and revise the system prompt to produce concise summaries with reference footnotes instead of inline citations. Monthly spend dropped from $12,000 to $3,200 within the first week, with no reduction in user-rated quality.

They also discovered that contract review tasks on short contracts (under 5 pages) were using the same claude-opus-4-5 model as long contracts. Routing short-contract reviews to claude-haiku-4-5 reduced per-task cost by 94% for that category.
Tracking cost only at billing time.
Provider invoices arrive daily or weekly. Real-time tracking is essential for catching runaway agents before they generate a large bill.
When the provider updates pricing, a single centralized price table is trivially updated; prices scattered through the codebase cause incorrect cost estimates.
If you use prompt caching, cached prefix tokens cost significantly less (typically 10% of full price). Your cost model must account for cache hit rates or it will overestimate cost by a large factor.
When an orchestrator spawns sub-agents, each sub-agent must carry its own budget slice and report cost back. A flat budget on the orchestrator only catches total spend, not per-sub-agent runaway.
Setting budgets too high during development.
Development agents with $100 budgets will train engineers to be careless about cost. Set dev budgets at $0.10–$0.50 so cost surprises are caught early.
A daily spend dashboard without anomaly detection still requires a human to notice the spike. Configure alerts for spend rate exceeding 3× the 7-day average.
Maintain one canonical price table module imported everywhere. Version it with the date prices were last verified.
each LLM call, not only after. Checking after the call prevents only the
call; checking before prevents paying for an output that cannot be used.
Use token count from the API response (
) for post-call cost recording, not a client-side estimate. The server count is authoritative.
at minimum. These three dimensions cover most diagnostic queries.
Log a structured cost summary on every task completion — even in development. These logs are the fastest path to understanding unit economics.
Run cost-per-task benchmarks on each model before deploying a new model version. Some models are faster and cheaper for specific task types; benchmark before assuming.
Implement graceful degradation in the circuit breaker: when a session budget is exhausted, return a partial result with a clear budget-exceeded status rather than raising an unhandled exception.

The cost tracking hot path — computing cost from token counts and checking against a session budget — runs in microseconds and adds negligible latency to an LLM call that itself takes hundreds of milliseconds. The cost is dominated by the arithmetic and a single dictionary lookup.
Scaling cost observability to thousands of concurrent agents requires:
A per-process in-memory ledger does not work when the same session spans multiple agent pods. Use Redis with an atomic
to maintain a shared session cost counter that all pods update.
At 1,000 concurrent tasks each making 10 LLM calls, your metrics pipeline receives 10,000 cost data points per minute. OTel's
aggregates these into counters before export — the pipeline load is proportional to the export interval, not the event rate.
Store cost metrics in Prometheus or a managed time-series store (Grafana Cloud, Datadog). These systems handle high-cardinality label sets and long-range queries efficiently.
Vendor-neutral; supports counters, histograms, gauges with label attribution
Tracks token costs per run automatically for LangChain-based agents
OTel-based; shows cost per span with model-aware pricing tables
Custom dashboards; alerting via Alertmanager; no built-in LLM cost support
Provider-invoice-based; delays attribution; excellent for cross-cloud cost management
Native LLM cost dashboards; integrates with Datadog APM traces
INCRBYFLOAT for atomic cross-pod session cost accumulation

The budget check must live in a layer that the agent code cannot bypass. Implement it as middleware above the agent loop, not inside the agent itself. An agent with a bug in its error handling could inadvertently skip an internal budget check.
with a Lua script that checks-and-increments atomically. Non-atomic updates allow two pods to both pass the budget check simultaneously and together overshoot the limit.

A price table that is months out of date will produce systematically incorrect cost estimates. Implement a price-table freshness check that alerts if the table has not been verified in the last 30 days.

If the cost tracking system itself fails (Redis unreachable, price table corrupt), fail open with conservative fallback behavior — either halt all LLM calls or allow them through with a warning log but no budget enforcement, based on your risk tolerance.
User IDs in metric labels are acceptable; full user email or name is not. Pseudonymize or hash user identifiers before attaching them to metrics exported to third-party observability backends.
Think of cost observability for agents as a

. When you get in a taxi, the meter starts. You know the rate per kilometer. At any point during the ride you can look at the meter and decide whether to continue or stop. If the fare reaches a pre-agreed limit, the driver stops — the trip ends there, not at the next convenient location.

The CostLedger is the meter. The per-token price table is the rate. The budget is the agreed fare limit. The circuit breaker is the driver who stops the car. The attribution labels (user, agent type, task category) are the receipt that tells you

Without the meter, you only know the total bill after the entire trip — which, for an agent that ran for an hour and looped fifty times, could be very large. With the meter, every increment is visible, attributable, and controllable in real time.
Agentic AI cost is unbounded by default — every LLM call, tool invocation, and loop iteration adds to the bill with no automatic stopping point.
Track cost per LLM call immediately from
each LLM call so the circuit breaker fires before incurring the next cost, not after.
Attribute every cost data point with
A centralized price table module is the single most important organizational practice — stale or duplicated prices make all cost estimates unreliable.
In distributed deployments, use Redis with atomic operations for cross-pod session budget enforcement — per-process ledgers do not prevent parallel-call budget overshoot.
Run cost-per-task benchmarks across models before routing decisions — a 94% cost reduction by routing short tasks to a smaller model is only visible through systematic cost observability.
